guest_info.name: Norman Ohler
guest_info.title: Author
expertise: History of drugs in Nazi Germany
Psychoactive drugs and World War II
History of human civilization through the lens of drugs

summary: Norman Ohler discusses the role of psychoactive drugs, particularly methamphetamine, in Nazi Germany and World War II. He explores Hitler's drug use, the Wehrmacht's use of Pervitin, and the broader context of drug use in Germany during that era, as well as his new book, Stoned Sapiens.

main_entities.name: Adolf Hitler
main_entities.context: Discussed in relation to his drug use, decision-making, and leadership during World War II.

main_entities.name: Theodor Morell
main_entities.context: Hitler's personal physician who administered drugs and other substances to him.

main_entities.name: Pervitin
main_entities.context: A brand-name methamphetamine drug used by the German military during the Blitzkrieg.

main_entities.name: Methamphetamine
main_entities.context: The psychoactive drug used by the German army to enhance performance and reduce fatigue.

main_entities.name: Blitzkrieg
main_entities.context: The German military strategy that was potentially influenced by methamphetamine use.

main_entities.name: Wehrmacht
main_entities.context: The German army during World War II, which systematically used methamphetamine.

main_entities.name: Eukodal
main_entities.context: An opioid drug (oxycodone) that Hitler used, administered by Morell.

main_entities.name: Stoned Sapiens
main_entities.context: Norman Ohler's upcoming book examining human history through the lens of drugs.

main_entities.name: Operation Barbarossa
main_entities.context: The German invasion of the Soviet Union, during which Hitler began using Dolantin.

main_entities.name: Sachsenhausen
main_entities.context: Concentration camp where the German Navy conducted drug experiments.

main_entities.name: The Bohemians
main_entities.context: Book by Norman Ohler about German resistance against the Nazis.

main_entities.name: Dunkirk
main_entities.context: Turning point in the Western campaign where Hitler halted the tanks, possibly influenced by Goering's morphine-high vision.

main_entities.name: Iboga
main_entities.context: Plant with psychoactive properties, potentially used by early humans to develop consciousness.

key_details.fact: The Versailles Treaty unfairly blamed Germany for World War I, leading to economic hardship and drug use in Berlin.
Hitler initially abstained from alcohol, caffeine, and meat, promoting an image of purity and discipline.
Temmler Company developed Pervitin (methamphetamine) to create a German performance enhancer.
Professor Ranke convinced the German military to use methamphetamine to combat fatigue and improve soldier performance.
Hitler's decision to split troops during Operation Barbarossa was influenced by his first opioid injection (Dolantin).
Morell created Hammer Pharmaceuticals to produce dubious hormonal concoctions for Hitler.
After the bomb attack in 1944, Hitler began using cocaine to cope with injuries and paranoia.
The German resistance group led by Harro Schulze-Boysen and Libertas used parties to recruit members.
Moses's vision of the burning bush and the Ten Commandments may have been influenced by DMT from acacia trees.

topics: The role of drugs in religion
The degeneration of Hitler as a leader

other_persons_mentioned.name: Ian Kershaw
other_persons_mentioned.context: Historian who described Ohler's work as a serious piece of scholarship.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Antony Beevor
other_persons_mentioned.context: Historian who described Ohler's work as a remarkable work of research.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Richard Evans
other_persons_mentioned.context: Historian who criticized Ohler's work as crass and inaccurate.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Hans Mommsen
other_persons_mentioned.context: German historian who mentored Ohler on his book Blitzed.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Fritz Hauschild
other_persons_mentioned.context: Chemist at Temmler Company who created methamphetamine.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Albert Speer
other_persons_mentioned.context: Hitler's architect and later arms minister.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Erwin Rommel
other_persons_mentioned.context: German general whose division used a lot of meth during the French campaign.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Winston Churchill
other_persons_mentioned.context: British Prime Minister who called the German storm through the Ardennes Mountains the sickle cut.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Hermann Göring
other_persons_mentioned.context: Head of the Luftwaffe and morphine addict who influenced Hitler's decision at Dunkirk.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Heinrich Böll
other_persons_mentioned.context: German Nobel Prize winner who wrote about the effects of Pervitin.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Keesing
other_persons_mentioned.context: Ear, nose, and throat specialist who treated Hitler after the bomb attack and introduced him to cocaine.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Heinrich Himmler
other_persons_mentioned.context: Head of the SS who was suspicious of Morell.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Harro Schulze-Boysen
other_persons_mentioned.context: Main figure in the German resistance movement, The Bohemians.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Libertas Schulze-Boysen
other_persons_mentioned.context: Wife of Harro and key figure in the German resistance movement.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Thomas Mann
other_persons_mentioned.context: Writer who left Germany and criticized the Nazis from abroad.

other_persons_mentioned.name: Terence McKenna
other_persons_mentioned.context: Proponent of the stoned ape theory.
